page: Google Cloud Platform n4d-highcpu-2 is an x86_64 architecture virtual machine instance within the n4d server family. It features 2 dedicated virtual CPUs (vCPUs) paired with 4.0 GB of system memory, yielding a resource density of 2.0 GB of RAM per core. This configuration does not include local NVMe storage or bundled GPU accelerators, and it provides zero complimentary public IPv4 addresses.

The instance utilizes dedicated CPU allocation, ensuring consistent processing capabilities without the performance fluctuations associated with shared-core or burstable instances. With a 1:2 vCPU-to-RAM ratio, the hardware profile is structured for compute-focused tasks that require dedicated processing power but have modest memory footprints. This resource density offers a cost-efficient option for workloads that do not require large memory allocations or local storage, avoiding the overhead of unutilized RAM.

Given the dedicated dual-core processing and 4.0 GB memory capacity, this instance is suited for lightweight compute-bound workloads. Appropriate use cases include small-scale web serving, microservices, basic CI/CD pipeline runners, and utility scripting. The lack of local storage and GPU acceleration makes it less suitable for database hosting, data science, or machine learning inference tasks.
description: Google Cloud Platform n4d-highcpu-2 is an x86_64 architecture virtual machine instance belonging to the n4d family. It is configured with 2 dedicated vCPUs and 4.0 GB of system memory, establishing a 2.0 GB memory-per-core ratio. The instance does not include local storage or GPU hardware, and it offers zero complimentary public IPv4 addresses. Featuring dedicated CPU allocation, the server provides stable, predictable compute performance without the resource contention typical of burstable instances. This resource profile is qualitatively cost-efficient for workloads requiring dedicated processing power with minimal memory overhead. The n4d-highcpu-2 is suited for lightweight compute-focused applications, including microservices, small web servers, and basic CI/CD tasks.
